title: Chinese Marching Band
excerpt: The sun had already risen. I opened one eye cautiously, and upon recognizing the blank walls of my room, I felt safe enough to open the other.
body:
The sun had already risen. I opened one eye cautiously, and upon recognizing the blank walls of my room, I felt safe enough to open the other. But this was a mistake, as soon after waves of pain thrashed against the sides of my skull, and with it came sporadic memories from the evening prior. Noise began to flood through my room, putting this odd mix of memories to a halt. 8 a.m., Thursday morning—cymbals clashed, trumpets blared, and snare drums hit hard, a Chinese marching band played outside a funeral home. Every Thursday I’d watch from my window like a gargoyle in disdain, slamming it shut in hopes it would muffle the sound a bit, though it never did.

It was the end of Spring, 2023. I had moved from LA to New York on a whim a few months prior. My apartment was a five-floor walk up perched atop the corner of Allen and Canal, across from the Nine Orchard Hotel. I could barely afford my rent but I wasn’t worried. As I was settling in to the city someone told me not to be concerned, as you either figure it out, or you don’t and you leave. My room was a shoebox with my bed wall to wall, with one side touching the radiator pipe. Having moved in the dead of winter, I conditioned myself to sleep completely still so I didn’t get burned by the pipe that lined that wall. I genuinely do not understand how that’s legal, but if New York City teaches you anything, it’s to not ask questions when it comes to your living situation. The lease agreement asked for 40x the rent, of which I did not make, and I signed the contract hoping for the best. After signing, the leasing office reached back out to me and asked me to get a guarantor to assist. Having already signed and moved my two suitcases up the stairs, I ignored the email.

My bedroom window faced floors of well-lit hotel rooms that I’d often find myself staring towards, wondering who was staying there and why. Having since stayed at the Nine Orchard, I can tell you it’s just a bunch of Europeans and Art Director types that do what they wish, often in an unmemorable manner. But the hotel is beautiful and clean, and the staff place a cookie on the bedside table during their turndown service, which means more to me than I’d like to admit, but what is a woman without her desires.

New to the city I naturally spent the next six months on a bender. The tone of those months were: Excess, Joy, Love, Pain, and an occasional game of Chess, in an attempt to keep my mind sharp. My weeks began on Wednesday, at what was at the time known as a chess club. Though not great at the game, I kept up in other ways. Wednesday nights stretched on until Sunday. In between those days I spent a lot of time drinking tequila sodas with lime and listening to the cocaine people speak and ketamine people laugh.

I befriended more criminals in my first six months living in the city than I had in my entire life. Dealers, white collar thieves, and degenerate gamblers. Men were scamming other men for money on the internet, and I was just happy to help them spend it. They were harmless, ambitious, and threw around advice and 100 dollar bills with a charming lack of self awareness. One told me that he hadn’t filed taxes in three years. Another mentioned that he was down 50,000 and had to go home. A few nights later he won 200,000 in front of me. His happiness was fleeting, and I came to the realization the money was as well. I don’t recall much from this time—but I do remember sitting in the more private dining area in a Japanese restaurant that no longer exists. After ordering a few rolls and filling up on drinks, someone at the table asked the waitress to close the curtains that separated us from the rest of the room. She happily obliged almost as if knowing what came next. He dumped a bag on the table and gestured to the pile. With grace, she arched over, used his credit card to cut a line, and took her fair share.

I was quickly inserted into the circuit of underground information: Where to eat, which gallery to check out, readings, parties, who to call when you need help, who to avoid, who to text for list, and when and where there was an open bar. Magnificently, I kept a job throughout this time, managing to attend Monday through Friday without getting fired. My walks from my apartment to the office in Soho were immensely valuable to my wellbeing, and some of the only times I felt a semblance of peace during this period. As long as I made it at a reasonable hour— this being before noon—I believed I was making real progress in a city that made no real sense to me yet.

The excess raged on into warmer weather. The final straw was a night that I found myself in a beautiful white woman’s opium den, furnished with pieces sourced from Facebook Marketplace. A long enough look around and I had no interest in taking part in any of it. Perhaps I was just in the wrong room at the wrong time, but I couldn’t ever let that happen again. It wasn’t the depravity that made me want to stop, but it was the room of conversations that didn’t mean a thing. Going from exuberant to miserable to then angry culminating in a five a.m. walk home smelling of jasmine and cigarettes, dodging rats that made me feel I was in enemy territory. I lay awake in bed that night letting tears hit my pillow case until I slept, waiting for my Marching band to wake me in time for work in three hours. The next day, I sat in an office chair at work shaking again, knowing the ride had peaked, at least for now. When the drugs become more interesting than the room, it’s over.

By sheer force I locked myself in my apartment for a while to get straight. If someone vanishes for a few weeks, they are either trying to write something, getting sober, or running out of money. I watched from my window and decided that I must do something completely normal before I put myself in any compromising scenarios, so I made a dentist appointment. The moment you make a health-related appointment that benefits the human body over the soul, you are a real citizen, no longer shiny and new, but seasoned and well-adjusted to the place you call home.

7:50 am, I was up and out of the house with enough time to grab a coffee and smoke a cigarette before getting my teeth scrubbed clean. It was early enough in the day that the air was crisp and had a slight bite. I crossed Allen and began my journey to the train, noticing storefronts that I could only previously recognize when the sun set. And there they were. Twenty or so Chinese people stepping out of the funeral home—my Chinese marching band—with instruments a plenty. Each band member looked over 50, with grins on their faces, chatting while lining up to perform. I took my headphones out and listened. Cymbals clashed, trumpets blared, and snare drums hit hard, and I was glad I could hear the music.
